messages.content: A month ago, I used AlphaEvolve to develop two predictors (in Python) for this challenge, which had good success rate on random instances of the problem, and were used to help develop the "hard" sets. The two predictors can be found [here](https://github.com/teorth/equational_theories/blob/main/scripts/predictor/predictor1.py) and [here](https://github.com/teorth/equational_theories/blob/main/scripts/predictor/predictor2.py), and were also [discussed in this post in the ETP zulip](https://leanprover.zulipchat.com/#narrow/channel/458659-Equational/topic/An.20implication.20predictor). It appears that the current cheatsheets are already well surpassing the capability of these initial predictors, so we will have to work harder to create a more meaningfully challenging evaluation set for this Stage 1 competition! But perhaps these predictors will be useful tools for competitors to generate further challenge data sets, which they are very welcome to share here!

@_**Terence Tao|28** [said](https://zulip.sair.foundation/#narrow/channel/13-Math-Distillation-Challenge---equational-theories/topic/Two.20python.20predictors/near/644):
```quote
A month ago, I used AlphaEvolve to develop two predictors (in Python) for this challenge, which had good success rate on random instances of the problem, and were used to help develop the "hard" sets. The two predictors can be found [here](https://github.com/teorth/equational_theories/blob/main/scripts/predictor/predictor1.py) and [here](https://github.com/teorth/equational_theories/blob/main/scripts/predictor/predictor2.py), and were also [discussed in this post in the ETP zulip](https://leanprover.zulipchat.com/#narrow/channel/458659-Equational/topic/An.20implication.20predictor). It appears that the current cheatsheets are already well surpassing the capability of these initial predictors, so we will have to work harder to create a more meaningfully challenging evaluation set for this Stage 1 competition! But perhaps these predictors will be useful tools for competitors to generate further challenge data sets, which they are very welcome to share here!
```

This is absolutely fascinating if it generalizes. Have you tested this on larger terms, different generators, families not seen in evolution or even just a few hand-crafted adversarial cases?

Would love to know if this is evidence of a universal implication method or if it's just learned the dataset geometry extremely well.
That's great news haha, So whose beating the back-end then eh?

@_**Terence Tao|28** [said](https://zulip.sair.foundation/#narrow/channel/13-Math-Distillation-Challenge---equational-theories/topic/Two.20python.20predictors/near/644):
```quote
A month ago, I used AlphaEvolve to develop two predictors (in Python) for this challenge, which had good success rate on random instances of the problem, and were used to help develop the "hard" sets. The two predictors can be found [here](https://github.com/teorth/equational_theories/blob/main/scripts/predictor/predictor1.py) and [here](https://github.com/teorth/equational_theories/blob/main/scripts/predictor/predictor2.py), and were also [discussed in this post in the ETP zulip](https://leanprover.zulipchat.com/#narrow/channel/458659-Equational/topic/An.20implication.20predictor). It appears that the current cheatsheets are already well surpassing the capability of these initial predictors, so we will have to work harder to create a more meaningfully challenging evaluation set for this Stage 1 competition! But perhaps these predictors will be useful tools for competitors to generate further challenge data sets, which they are very welcome to share here!
```

How in practices does the process of curating the hardest possible and widely distributed dataset from all 22 million magmas looks like? 

my GPT cheatsheets definitely do not generalize very well across the datasets (hard1, hard2 and the list from Theort) yet in each of them they are easily findable, and the model can saturate the benchmark. There is something weird going on with the distribution of hard magmas. But I am not a mathematician!

You are a mathematician specializing in equational theories of magmas!!
